Mechanistic interpretability · meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct

The User Axis

A language model quietly infers who it is talking to and adapts. We recovered that inference as a single direction in activation space — interpretable and steerable — then asked the headline question: does a user's position on this axis predict how far the model's own persona drifts? It doesn't, and the way that link dissolves under scrutiny is the point.

competent · bounded vulnerable · emotional
150
user personas
7,200
rollouts captured
80

1
dominant axis

link criteria met

An interactive summary of the User-Axis study (Stages A–F). All figures and numbers are drawn from the run artifacts; charts are built from the released projection tables.

Stages A · B — the data

150 users, one shared interview, two ways of asking

We generated a pool of 150 user personas spanning five latent traits — expertise, vulnerability, trust, emotional load, and technical literacy — plus age bracket and domain. Every persona faced the same 24 persona-agnostic probes, so any activation difference reflects the user, not the question.

Each persona was elicited two ways: an explicit arm (a system prompt describing the user) and an implicit arm (the persona speaks in its own voice). That yields 150 × 24 × 2 = 7,200 rollouts. A Stage-D filter keeps a rollout only when the model's own verbalized guess about the user matches the intended traits — 6,770 / 7,200 (94%) survive.

Readout · primaryresp_mean
[80 × 8192]
Mean residual-stream activation over every token of the model's response, at all 80 layers.
Readout · secondarylast_user
[80 × 8192]
Activation at the final user token, before the model has said a word — its pre-response user model.

Both readouts are captured in a single forward pass with per-layer hooks that reduce inside the hook, never materializing the full sequence tensor.

Stages C · E — the method

Mirror the Assistant Axis — onto the user

Lu et al.'s Assistant Axis measures how far the model's own persona sits from its trained default, by varying the model's role. We flip the pipeline: hold the model fixed, vary the user, and read the model's internal representation of that user.

  • Capture — per-persona mean activation vectors over its kept rollouts, per readout and per arm.
  • Decompose — PCA per layer over the 150 persona vectors; the leading component PC1 is the User Axis.
  • Cross-check — compare PC1 against an independent vulnerability-contrast vector, mean(high-vuln) − mean(low-vuln·expert).
  • Anchor — all readouts reported at layer ℓ=40 (the Assistant-Axis target layer for this model).
Result I — discovery

A single, low-dimensional, interpretable User Axis

One direction dominates, and it is exactly the hypothesized vulnerable / emotional ↔ competent / expert contrast. Toggle the readout to see it on both the response-token and the pre-response representations.

PC1 correlation with held-out traits
PC1 variance
20.4%
of persona-vector variance in one direction; 70% within 18 PCs.
Contrast cosine @ℓ40
0.933
alignment with the independent vulnerability-contrast axis (best 0.961).
TraitPC1 loading correlationrq (FDR)
Elicitation invariance
Explicit and implicit arms recover the same persona ordering.
r = 0.686
Result II — causality

The axis drives behavior, not just tracks it

Adding ±α·û to the residual stream at ℓ=40 — with no persona context — produces monotone, judge-detectable shifts. Drag the coefficient from the competent pole to the vulnerable pole.

−8 competent vulnerable +8
α = 0
Warmth ↑, technical density ↓ move cleanly (Spearman ρ = +0.88 / −1.00) while coherence stays high (8.8–9.7): steering shapes register without breaking the model. Protectiveness and caution don't move reliably — a partial but real control result.

Stage F — the headline test

Does User-Axis position predict Assistant drift?

The motivating claim: vulnerable users push the model's persona off its default. Our User Axis is a vulnerability axis on the input side — so we can test it directly. Define drift = −(â·r) (higher = more drifted from the Assistant default) and correlate it with the user's position on the axis.

The hazard. The User Axis û and the Assistant Axis â live in the same residual space. Read a turn's user-position and its drift off the same vector and their correlation is partly mechanical — fixed by the angle between the axes, not by any real prediction. So we tier the test by how much representation each predictor shares with drift.

Tier 1 — the axes are nearly orthogonal

At ℓ=40, cos(û, â) = +0.0001 — inside the ±0.033 random band. The mechanical floor is essentially zero, so any circular correlation is coming from the response's content covariance, not axis overlap.

Tiers 2–3 — the link collapses as you de-circularize

Each bar is the partial correlation of a user-position predictor with drift, controlling length, arm, and probe. As the predictor is read from representations sharing less with drift, the strong positive signal collapses and flips negative. The fully independent trait tag shows no positive effect at all.

User position → drift · partial r @ℓ40
positive (supports link) negative (against link)
Circular readsame vector
+0.223
The naive analysis that would have "confirmed" the hypothesis.
Cross-representationindependent token
−0.163
Read the user model on the pre-response token: the effect reverses.

It holds across every layer

The circular signal (blue) decays with depth and crosses zero; the cross-representation (crimson) and the independent vulnerability tag (amber) stay at or below zero throughout. Hover for values.

userpos_resp — circular userpos_lastB — cross-rep β vulnerability tag

And the traits that do predict drift aren't about vulnerability at all — it's a competence story:

expertise → drift
+0.39
q < 10⁻³ · more expert users → more drift
tech_literacy → drift
−0.45
q < 10⁻³ · more tech-literate → less drift
LINK NOT SUPPORTED 0 / 3 Pre-registered criteria for the headline link, all failed:
✗Tier 2. Independent vulnerability tag → drift is positive & FDR-significant. Actual: −0.05, q=0.54 (null).
✗Tier 3. Cross-representation partial positive, significant, attenuated. Actual: −0.16 — significant but wrong sign.
✗Consistency. Same positive sign across both arms and the layer grid. Actual: negative everywhere.
The axis reading is trustworthy. The published Assistant Axis reproduces from its own components at cosine 0.99997; our extractor recovers its sign on held-out case studies (2/2 capped ≫ unsteered); an independent from-scratch recompute matches at cosine 0.86. The null is a real finding, not a broken measurement.

What to keep

Takeaways

  • A real, low-dimensional, interpretable User Axis exists on Llama-3.3-70B — PC1 tracks vulnerability at r up to 0.86, stable across elicitation and readout.
  • It is behaviorally causal: steering along it cleanly moves warmth and technical register while preserving coherence.
  • But the headline predictive link to Assistant drift does not hold. The positive correlation is a shared-representation artifact; de-circularization is not optional.
  • Where drift is predictable, it's a competence signal (expertise, tech-literacy) — not vulnerability.
  • Caveats: one model; a projection-only (not generation-time) test of drift; and a broad persona pool rather than the acute emotionally-manipulative case the original claim emphasizes.
User-Axis study · Stages A–F · Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct (80 layers, d=8192, ℓ=40) · readouts resp_mean + last_user · methodology mirrors the Assistant Axis (Lu et al.).
Numbers from the run's analysis/*.json and projection tables · full derivations in the ICLR technical report.
